food_subgroup: Grilled beef rice bowl with egg and soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for beef yakiniku bowl combo; mapped to 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002809) as the closest parent class. A more specific donburi or yakiniku term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a composite code for beef yakiniku bowl combo. A0FJP (cooked beef) is used as an exact match for the primary beef ingredient anchor. Composite dish coding is not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for beef yakiniku bowl or donburi-style composite dishes.

main_ingredients: yakiniku sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, sesame)

serving.description: 1 bowl combo (beef yakiniku bowl + half-boiled egg + miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese short-grain rice topped with grilled marinated beef slices, one half-boiled egg, and a side of miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Yakiniku (grilled marinated beef) with steamed white rice, egg, and miso soup — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 172459); and University of Sydney GI Database for glycemic index.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for beef yakiniku bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 150 g grilled marinated beef (27%), 280 g cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (51%), 50 g half-boiled egg (9%), and 70 g miso soup (13%) per 550 g serving, then back-calculated to per-100 g basis. Yakiniku sauce contribution (soy, mirin, sugar, sesame) is incorporated into the beef component estimate. USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used for each component. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant values may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Cooked white rice (Japanese short-grain) GI = 68 (glucose reference). Composite dish GL calculated from carbohydrate content of full serving.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef yakiniku bowl combo as a composite dish. The GI of 68 is assigned based on the dominant glycemic driver being Japanese short-grain cooked white rice, for which GI = 68 (glucose scale) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The beef, egg, and miso soup components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (68 × 101.75) / 100 ≈ 69.2, rounded to 69.3. Available carbohydrate per serving estimated as 18.5 g/100g × 550 g / 100 = 101.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from yakiniku sauce (soy sauce base) and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium yakiniku sauce and omit or reduce miso soup, as a standard serving provides an estimated 1,705 mg sodium (~74% of the WHO 2,300 mg daily limit).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large portion of Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 68, GL ~69 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving carries a high glycemic load of approximately 69, driven by the substantial white rice component; reducing rice portion size, pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables, or substituting brown rice where available can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from sauce and miso soup
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the moderate saturated fat content (~7.7 g per serving) and high sodium load (~1,705 mg per serving); choosing leaner beef cuts, requesting lighter sauce application, and omitting miso soup can reduce both saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yakiniku Bowl Combo is a Japanese-style donburi (rice bowl) dish consisting of thick-cut beef slices marinated in yakiniku sauce — a sweet-savoury Japanese barbecue sauce typically made from soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, and sesame — that are grilled and served over a generous portion of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The combo is completed with a half-boiled egg (ajitsuke tamago-style or plain) and a side of miso soup or a beverage. The dish is a popular fast-casual Japanese meal format widely served in gyudon and yakiniku chain restaurants across Japan and Singapore. The beef is tender and caramelised from the grilling process, with a glossy, umami-rich coating from the yakiniku sauce. The rice is soft and slightly sticky, characteristic of Japanese short-grain varieties. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-to-high calorie meal providing approximately 814 kcal per standard 550 g serving, with meaningful protein (~50.6 g), moderate fat (~22.6 g), and a high carbohydrate load (~101.8 g) dominated by the rice component. The glycemic load per serving is high (~69), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-sauce-based yakiniku marinade and miso soup, raising concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, egg, wheat, or sesame allergies. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Japanese cuisine and a common lunch or dinner option in Singapore's Japanese restaurant segment.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for beef yakiniku bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for each component (grilled beef, cooked white rice, boiled egg, miso soup). The GI is assigned by analogy to Japanese short-grain cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly regarding the exact yakiniku sauce formulation and restaurant-specific portion sizes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates assembled from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source record for beef yakiniku bowl combo exists. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese fast-casual donburi combo portions; actual restaurant serving weights at Sukiya Singapore and Funan Mix & Match may differ. 3. Yakiniku sauce sodium contribution is estimated from typical soy-sauce-based yakiniku sauce formulations; exact sauce recipe is proprietary. 4. The GI of 68 is assigned by analogy to Japanese short-grain cooked white rice (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not directly reported for this composite dish. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; yakiniku sauce may contain mirin (alcohol) and the beef sourcing at these Singapore outlets is not confirmed halal-certified. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON include 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', 'Fried Chicken', and 'Drinks & Beverages' which are not relevant to this dish and appear to be platform-level category tags rather than dish-specific descriptors; these have been disregarded. 8. FoodOn match is closest_parent (beef dish); no exact FoodOn term for yakiniku bowl exists. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (cooked beef) is used as an exact match for the primary ingredient anchor only. 9. No -1 sentinel values are used for core macros; all secondary nutrient -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data as documented above.
